title: Building an Industrial System
concept: Deploying industrial vision systems requires managing camera placement, lighting, triggering, and processing speed constraints.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of multispectral imaging in agriculture?

options.text: It captures invisible light bands that show plant health and stress
It makes model graphs smaller to run

exercises.explain: Multispectral cameras capture wavelengths like near-infrared that reveal plant chlorophyll activity.

cards.title: Control what you can
cards.body: When you deploy AI in the wild, you deal with unpredictable sun, rain, and occlusion. But inside a factory, you are the boss! You can install custom lights, position cameras exactly, and control the conveyor speed. Industrial vision is all about designing the environment for success!

cards.title: Industrial Inspection Systems
cards.body: An industrial vision system checks manufactured items for defects on a conveyor belt. It requires precise illumination (e.g., ring lights to eliminate shadows), physical camera enclosures, and hardware triggers.

cards.title: A photo studio vs. street photography
cards.body: Outdoor AI is like street photography: you must adapt to whatever happens. Industrial AI is like a professional photo studio: you set the lights, backdrop, and camera angles so every single shot looks identical.

cards.title: Defect detection on a production line
cards.body: A bottling plant uses a camera looking down on bottle caps. Backlighting highlights cap shape. A defect model checks 50 bottles per second, instantly triggering a pneumatic arm to eject misaligned caps.

cards.title: Code is not the most important part
cards.body: You might think building industrial vision is mostly about writing deep neural networks. In practice, setting up correct lighting (e.g., dome lights to prevent glare on metal parts) does 90% of the work to make the AI's job easy.

cards.title: Designing the Environment
cards.body: Comparing environment setups for model accuracy:

left.text:
Fixed overhead lighting
Constant conveyor speed
Guaranteed camera distance
High accuracy, simple models

right.text:
Changing solar glare
Moving targets
Random target distances
Complex models, lower reliability

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of controlled lighting (like ring lights or backlights) in industrial vision systems?

options.text: To create consistent, high-contrast images and eliminate shadows
To speed up the backpropagation learning process
To reduce the file size of the exported ONNX model

exercises.explain: Controlled lighting ensures consistent inputs, which makes predictions highly reliable.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a factory, physical camera enclosures are required to protect lenses from dust, vibrations, and water.
exercises.explain: True. Industrial settings are harsh; enclosures protect camera hardware from damage.

exercises.sentence: An electronic signal that tells a camera to capture a photo the exact millisecond an object passes on a conveyor belt is a ___.
exercises.explain: Triggers synchronize image capture with physical object arrival.

exercises.prompt: Which setup is best for inspecting shiny metal screws on a fast conveyor belt?

options.text: A dome light to diffuse reflection and prevent shiny hot-spots
Direct, harsh spotlights that create deep shadows
No lighting, relying on normal warehouse light bulbs

exercises.explain: Dome lights diffuse light evenly, preventing glare on metallic surfaces.

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to inspect microchips for scratches. The chips move past at 30 chips per second. The camera is perfectly positioned, but reflections on the silicon make scratches invisible in raw photos. What should you change first?

options.text: Adjust the lighting angles (e.g. use low-angle darkfield lighting) to make scratches glow against a dark chip background.
Swap the model for a 100-layer transformer backbone.
Run model pruning to make inference faster.
Increase the training data batch size.

exercises.explain: Next up: Modern Object Detection. Before modifying model code, resolving physics problems like lighting and reflection is the fastest and most effective way to improve industrial computer vision systems.
